invariant_definition: *Anumāna* is cognition generated through a sign-relation rather than direct presentation. Its classical Nyāya form presupposes *pakṣa* (the subject under consideration), *sādhya* (what is to be established), *hetu* or *liṅga* (the inferential mark), and *vyāpti* (invariable concomitance), typically secured through agreement in presence and absence across *sapakṣa* and *vipakṣa* cases. The public five-member form of inference is pedagogical; the deeper issue is what licenses the transition from observed sign to unobserved fact. Navya-Nyāya sharpens this with *avacchedaka* language; Buddhist logic recasts it through *trairūpya*; Vedānta uses it extensively in polemic while denying that *brahman* is inferable as just one more unperceived object. The term therefore belongs to a large inferential apparatus, not merely to 'reasoning' in general. In practice, *anumāna* governs everything from fire-from-smoke cases to anti-Sāṃkhya and anti-Buddhist arguments about causality, selfhood, and scriptural authority.

school_framing.shared_core: *Anumāna* names **inferential cognition: cognition of a *sādhya* by means of a *liṅga* whose *vyāpti* with the *sādhya* is established** (*Nyāya-Sūtra* 1.1.5; *Hetu-bindu*). All schools agree on the *liṅga-paramarśa* structure. They differ on the *vyāpti*-grounding (Buddhists: *svabhāva-* and *kārya-hetu* with *tādātmya*/*tad-utpatti*; Nyāya: *sāhacarya* + *upādhi-rāhitya*) and on the number of *avayava*s (Nyāya: 5; Buddhist: 2 or 3).
school_framing.register_axes_note: Same LD+E register, same scope-inside. Real disagreement on *vyāpti*-formation and *avayava*-count (AF1). Buddhist anti-realist construal of *sāmānya* shifts what *liṅga*-classes mean, but the inferential structure is shared.

per_school.definition: Advaita accepts anumāna among the operative vyāvahārika pramāṇas, but reserves final brahmajñāna to Veda as the sole pramāṇa in that matter (cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9). Inference and anvaya-vyatireka reasoning therefore serve to remove error, prepare sentence-meaning, and articulate non-dual teaching, without independently yielding the akhaṇḍārtha of brahman (cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.9-10).

Viśiṣṭādvaita deploys anumāna positively in siddhi-literature, as Yāmuna's Īśvara-Siddhi constructs a staged inference from the ordered, embodied, and composite character of the world to a single governing puruṣa (cite://yamuna/isvara-siddhi/16 cite://yamuna/isvara-siddhi/17 cite://yamuna/isvara-siddhi/18). At the same time, the school treats such inference as confirmatory rather than scripturally substitutive, since Brahman is finally known in harmony with śruti rather than as a merely inferable unperceived object (cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/1.1.1).

Tattva-vāda gives anumāna an aggressive polemical role, especially in dismantling rival inferences such as the Advaitin's mithyātvānumāna, whose pakṣa and qualifying terms Madhva attacks as aprasiddha (cite://madhva/tattvodyota/Anumānanirāsaḥ cite://madhva/tattvodyota/Pakṣa Bears an Unestablished Predicate). In later scholastic form it also internalizes Navya-Nyāya inferential machinery, as Vyāsatīrtha explicitly formulates svato-grāhyatva by full anumāna-structure (cite://vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda, first svato-grāhyatvānumāna).

Acintya-Bhedābheda does not reject inference, but it construes tarka and nyāya as subordinate to scriptural coordination when the śakti-śaktimat relation is at stake (cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42). Jīva Gosvāmī's paired reasoning removes contradiction only by preserving both difference and non-difference under acintya-śakti, so anumāna functions up to the point where exclusive bheda or abheda alternatives would misdescribe the object (cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43).

Śuddhādvaita employs reasoning in doctrinal exposition, but it denies that Brahman can be fixed by ordinary inferential categories such as partless or possessed-of-parts, since Brahman is vedaika-samadhigamya (cite://vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45c). Its use of yukti is therefore supportive and exegetical, for example when samanvaya and ātmakṛti are invoked to articulate avikṛta-pariṇāma without reducing Brahman to a mutable substrate (cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4b cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26a cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26b).

Sāṃkhya accepts three pramāṇas: dṛṣṭa, anumāna, and āptavacana (cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/4). It defines anumāna as liṅga-liṅgi-pūrvaka and assigns it a specific task in reaching atīndriya objects that are not available to perception (cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/5 cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/6). The Sāṃkhya-Sūtra gives the same pramāṇa a tighter logical definition as cognition of the correlated item from apprehended pratibandha (cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.100).

Yoga counts anumāna among the five cittavṛttis under the pramāṇa-class, alongside pratyakṣa and āgama (cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.7). Vyāsa defines it as the vṛtti whose core is a general ascertainment grounded in a relation that recurs among similar cases and is excluded from dissimilar ones, thereby giving Yoga an explicitly inferential epistemology within its soteriological psychology (cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.7).

Nyāya classifies anumāna as a threefold pramāṇa proceeding from what is preceded by a prior cognition of relation, namely pūrvavat, śeṣavat, and sāmānyato dṛṣṭa (cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.5). The later Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika digest tradition makes its efficient cause parāmarśa, defined as cognition of pakṣadharmatā qualified by vyāpti, and it distinguishes svārthānumāna from parārthānumāna in the standard smoke-fire case (cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/44 cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/45).

Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā does not deny anumāna, but it refuses to let it displace Vedic śabda in the ascertainment of dharma, whose object is supersensible and fixed by the sentence-meaning of codanā (cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.5). Śabara explicitly rejects the move from the fallibility of human utterance to an inference of Vedic fallibility, so inference here remains a secondary pramāṇa whose scope does not overrun apauruṣeya śabda (cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-anumana).

Mādhyamaka does not build its final position on a self-standing inferential thesis; Nāgārjuna instead blocks the conversion of emptiness into a svabhāva-backed sādhya, rejecting the opponent's hetu as sādhyasama while allowing only conventional transaction (cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/28). Since he claims no independent pratijñā of his own, anumāna is admitted only under saṃvyavahāra and not as an ultimately self-grounding instrument (cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/29).

In the Yogācāra-pramāṇa line, pramāṇa is twofold because the knowable is twofold, and inference belongs to the conceptual side rather than to bare svalakṣaṇa-perception (cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.1 cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.75). Dharmakīrti accordingly grounds anumāna in liṅga through anvaya-vyatireka, with the inferential mark functioning only where a non-deviant relation to the probandum has been secured (cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.67).

Pratyabhijñā accepts anumāna as reflective construction from a presently manifested sign toward what is not yet manifest, and Abhinavagupta glosses it precisely as such vimarśa (cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.8). Yet the same discussion denies that external objects are thereby established, so inference is real as a cognitive operation but limited with respect to siddhi of bāhyārtha (cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.9).

translator_note: The lazy gloss 'inference' is acceptable only if one does not equate it with a generic Aristotelian syllogism or with modern formal entailment. *Anumāna* is tied to *vyāpti*, sign-cognition, and a debated theory of how a universal relation is known. Translating it as mere 'reasoning' removes the entire inferential machinery the schools are arguing over.
